data.title: Cambodian beauty queen angers Thais over border row comment
data.description:
A Cambodian beauty queen has stirred controversy in Thailand after giving a political speech accusing Bangkok of provoking conflict during a pageant segment in Phnom Penh, with social media users accusing her of using an international dispute to grab attention.
A day after Chouri Laorhours was crowned Miss Teen Cambodia, her tearful speech was posted on the pageant’s social media account.
In a video, Laorhours carries a Cambodian flag and starts her speech in Khmer. Speaking “on behalf of all Cambodian people,” she asks for the return of 18 Cambodian soldiers being held by Thailand, according to the Khmer Times newspaper.
She then switches to English, adding: “Cambodia and Thailand have always lived together in peace, but now that peace has ended because Thailand started the war, something we never asked for … We do not want to fight. We want peace. We are not enemies. We are neighbours, human beings who deserve safety and a future free from hatred and war. Thank you.”
Soon after the clip was posted on Monday, it drew contrasting comments on both sides of the border.
Cambodians commended her for her patriotic speech. Thais, however, questioned the appropriateness of making such a political statement during a beauty pageant, while others accused her of fanning anti-Thai sentiments at such a sensitive time, The Nation reported. The phrase “Thailand started the war” is also trending online.
Tensions continue to rise between the two nations despite a US-brokered ceasefire agreement signed by them late last month.

On November 10, Thailand said it was suspending the deal and halting the release of 18 Cambodian soldiers in its custody after a landmine, said to have been recently planted by Cambodia, exploded and injured two Thai soldiers.
Since then, skirmishes have been reported, with the two sides accusing the other of shooting first.
Neither Thai officials nor the pageant organisers have issued a formal response to Laorhours’ speech, according to The Nation report.
Laorhours is no stranger to controversy. Throughout the campaign, she was criticised by social media users who said she was a perennial pageant queen because of her wealthy background, the Khmer Times reported.
She denied the accusations, saying that her achievements were the result of talent, experience and hard work.
“Those who think I win because I have money are wrong. I always compete based on my ability. The pageant chooses contestants who truly have talent. Whether someone has resources or not, if they have more ability, they will win the crown with dignity,” she posted on social media.
Beauty pageants have been under fresh scrutiny in Thailand, especially after negative reports about the ongoing Miss Universe pageant held in the kingdom.

Two judges have resigned after claiming the results were fixed, and Miss Universe Thailand director Nawat Itsaragrisil has been sidelined after he reportedly called Miss Mexico a “dumbhead”.
Miss Israel also said she received death threats after an edited video, purportedly showing her shooting a dirty look at Miss Palestine standing nearby, went viral.
“It’s not only death threats, but sexual assault threats,” Melanie Shiraz told the New York Post on Friday. “I experienced antisemitism before, but I didn’t think it would be this bad.”
Last month, Israel agreed to a deal with Hamas to end the Israel-Gaza war, but sporadic clashes continue to threaten the fragile peace.

data.title: ‘Unemployed penguin’: fans saddened by imminent retirement of Japan beloved smart card mascot
data.description:
The beloved penguin mascot of a transport card in Japan, regarded by many as the most popular penguin in the country, is expected to retire in 2027, according to its issuer.
The affable Suica Penguin will be hanging up its flippers at the end of the 2026 financial year, which concludes on March 31, 2027. It has graced the face of a widely used transport smart card since 2001, as announced by the Tokyo-based rail operator JR East on November 11, reported the Tokyo Weekender.
This card is the first transport Integrated Circuit (IC) card in Japan, with the name Suica derived from the acronym for Super Urban Intelligent Card.

The company revealed it has issued over 110 million such cards over the years, suggesting that nearly everyone in Tokyo has encountered this beloved penguin.
Locally, residents often refer to this card as the “watermelon card” because Suica sounds similar to the word for the fruit in Japanese.
Initially designed for train boarding, the card later evolved to include tapping functionality for payments at convenience stores.
As a result, the Suica Penguin has become the most recognisable cartoon image among tourists visiting Japan, thanks to the card’s extensive use.

This adorable penguin, an Adélie, a species from Antarctica, was created by Chiharu Sakazaki, a picture book artist and illustrator celebrated for her minimal yet charming and expressive style.
Sakazaki explained that the penguin was originally a character in her picture book before being discovered by the art director who developed the visual identity for the Suica card.
The penguin made its debut in posters promoting the card’s launch, and its overwhelming cuteness prompted the rail company to feature it on the card itself.
Since then, the penguin has gained such fame that it even has a statue located in Suica Penguin Square in Shinjuku, Tokyo.
Earlier this year, during a marketing event for the Suica card, the penguin’s belly was designed to protrude, inviting passers-by to touch it for stress relief. The company also stationed an employee near the penguin image to assist visitors in taking photographs.

JR East announced that a new character will take over, with plans to invite the public to participate in the selection process, although further details have yet to be revealed.
Many people have questioned the company’s decision, with some even petitioning for its reversal.
“I don’t think there’s any need for the penguin to ‘graduate.’ There must be some compelling reason behind this,” commented one internet user.
Another expressed sadness, saying, “I am heartbroken by this news. I will miss it…I feel the penguin on this card is becoming extinct, much like the dodo bird.”

A third user remarked: “I want the penguin to live on. Please don’t let Japan become less charming.”
A shopping website selling Suica Penguin merchandise experienced such high traffic that it took five minutes to access the site after the news of the mascot’s retirement broke.
The announcement also garnered attention on social media in China.
“I don’t understand why a mascot needs to retire. The penguin is so iconic. I feel sad about its retirement,” commented a mainland Chinese observer.

data.title: Inside the Hong Kong collectors’ society that treats its antiques like ‘babies’
data.description:
For Dr Maria Mok Kar-wing, staging an exhibition of more than 500 priceless items tickled her maternal instincts. “It was more like handling 500 babies,” says the director of the Hong Kong Museum of Art (HKMoA) of organising “Engaging Past Wisdom: Min Chiu Society at Sixty-five”. “The collectors are their parents and these treasures are their babies.”
Never mind that some of them are thousands of years old. The infants in question are, in fact, Chinese antiques, and their “parents” as such are members of the prestigious Min Chiu Society, a group of art collectors dedicated to the preservation and appreciation of classical Chinese arts and crafts.
Established in 1960 with 15 members including founding chairman Hu Jenmou, the Min Chiu Society intentionally operates under the radar. Besides its quinquennial exhibition, its collections and activities are not publicised.

“That is the intention: it’s a closed society, and we’re very low key,” says Lawrence Chan Wing-tat, chairman of the group. Passion for Chinese antiquities is a common factor among members, who come from various professional backgrounds and include industrialists, bankers, architects, doctors and lawyers. Chan himself has a background in advertising but is also known for his Dawentang Collection, which is highly regarded for its imperial porcelain from the Ming and Qing dynasties.
We’re seated in the museum’s conference room, sipping hot oolong tea from porcelain cups and looking out of the floor-to-ceiling windows at the uninterrupted view of Hong Kong Island across Victoria Harbour. It’s an unusual moment of reflection found in a typically fast-paced, anxiety-ridden Hong Kong day.
“Most of our activities are dedicated to learning about these antiques and preserving their history,” says Chan. “We’re intensely passionate about gaining knowledge.”

Bridging the gap between collecting and connoisseurship, real luxury is derived from appreciating an object’s exquisite craftsmanship and understanding the historical context in which it was conceived.
Hence the title, “Engaging Past Wisdom”, based on a Chinese idiom that translates to “learning from the past to understand the present”. The first character in the idiom is derived from a pictogram used in ancient China, representing a figure bent down over a basin of water looking at their own reflection.
The society has the kind of cultural cachet that results in its members being sought by institutions such as the Victoria and Albert and the British Museum in London and the Met in New York. And while on occasion they do lend their pieces to foreign institutions, the society’s relationship to the HKMoA in particular has stood the test of time.
“For Min Chiu collectors, they hold on to the belief that collecting is a passion; it’s not just about investment,” says Mok. “So when I compared the pieces to babies, I was joking, but not really. It’s something that they love and, for us, it’s about translating that love from the collector.”

Collecting art and belonging to the Min Chiu Society brought Chan and his wife together in more ways than one. The couple was introduced to the society by James Watt, a scholar and former curator at HKMoA.
“James travelled a lot to China in the 1960s and 70s and has many stories about the love of emperors for collecting antiquities,” says Chan. “I thought, well, if it was good enough for emperors, it’s good enough for me.”
The society’s clubhouse is now on Bowen Road and serves as a salon of sorts. Members convene for a monthly dinner at the premises, where they exchange thoughts, ideas and information. At times they invite academics, experts, museum curators and directors to speak. “In one simple flat you can see the collecting soft power that Hong Kong has,” says Mok. “It spans generations, too.”
The process to become a Min Chiu member is rigorous. While already knowing other members and having a deep interest in Chinese arts and culture are a given, potential candidates also need to undergo several rounds of meetings and interviews, and be vetted by a committee, whose decision needs to be unanimous.

Expanding and diversifying membership, however, is seen as key to cementing the society’s legacy and ensuring its scholarly work continues. Chan says women are now able to join the society, which has “definitely expanded the membership and visibility”.
Nurturing an appreciation among the public, particularly among the younger generations, for Chinese arts and antiquities is also high on the agenda. “We want the audience to walk away with the impression they witnessed the birth of Chinese art and culture, and have seen its beauty,” says Chan. “Maybe they’ll be inspired to acquire some pieces in the future.”
“Engaging Past Wisdom” marks the society’s 65th anniversary and diverges from standard chronological formats by embracing a more thematic curatorial approach. Divided into five chapters and an educational corner that documents the society’s history, the exhibition reflects the breadth and diversity of Chinese arts and antiquities spanning 5,000 years. The exhibits range from calligraphy, jade and bronze to wood furniture and sculptures, fabrics, lacquerware, glassware, weaponry and metalwork.
When I compared the pieces to babies, I was joking, but not really
Maria Mok Kar-wing
The first section, “Preserving the Past: Perceiving the Present”, showcases long-standing motifs in Chinese art. A more than 2,000-year-old vase, or “ritual vessel”, from the Zhou dynasty is placed next to a pale-blue Qing dynasty vase that was made almost two millennia later, mimicking the shape, form and even animal-motif handles of the older one and reflecting the enduring power of techniques and ideologies.
A pendant of a dancing robed woman with one hand on her head and the other on her hip appears behind the vessels. Dating back to the 3rd century BC, she appears to be almost floating. This attention to detail recurs through the displays. “We are really fussy with how we mount the objects,” says Mok.
The second section contains minimalist ceramics, typical of the Song dynasty, whereas the third section sees objects of clothing take centre stage, including a set of ceremonial robes from the Qing dynasty worn by the emperor. Traditionally, the numbers nine and five symbolised the ruler’s supreme authority and, as such, the robe was decorated with five dragons on the front, with an additional three on the back.

The dichotomy between craftsmanship and natural beauty informs the fourth section, where an intricately carved spinach-green jade table screen from the 18th century Qing dynasty depicting a tributary scene, is placed next to an untouched natural Lingbi stone from the county of the same name, in Anhui province. The objects “can be naturalistic, minimalistic or ornate and require a lot of craftsmanship”, says Mok.
One of Chan’s highlights is a Ming dynasty Yongle blue-and-white basin inspired by a Persian original.
The piece’s significance lies in its representation of the Silk Road trade at the time between China and Persia. The vibrancy of the glaze and pigment in particular attracted Chan, and while it took him some time to acquire it, he says he’s never encountered a similar piece on the market.
Another rare work on view, a Wucai covered jar in a “hundred boys” design dating back to the mid-17th century, happens to be the first substantial piece Chan acquired. Adorned with a traditional and auspicious motif that signifies abundance, the jar depicts a colourful and highly detailed scene in which boys are seen engrossed in various recreational activities.

“My wife and I became so fascinated by this piece that ever since we bought it, we have been hooked on collecting antiques,” says Chan, who acquired the jar at a Sotheby’s auction.
While Hong Kong maintains its status in the contemporary art market, it’s exhibitions such as “Engaging Wisdom” that keep the city at the forefront of classical Chinese art and antiquity scholarship.
“Contemporary art comes and goes,” says Chan, emphasising that the Min Chiu Society doesn’t entertain collections or collectors of digital, AI or 21st century art. “All kinds of Chinese antiquities and classical art will always be there, generation after generation …”

data.title: Russian mother warns Malaysians over box jellyfish threat after boy dies from sting
data.description:
A two-year-old Russian boy has died from complications after being stung by a box jellyfish at Chenang Beach in Langkawi, Malaysia’s Kedah state, following five days of fighting for his life.
Sinar Harian reported that Vladimir Lakubanets died on Wednesday at the Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital (HSB) in Alor Setar after suffering serious complications from the sting, which occurred last Saturday.
His father, Nikita, 32, said the incident happened suddenly while his son was playing in shallow water.
“My wife handed my child to me and within seconds, he stopped breathing. I performed CPR as quickly as possible and other tourists helped take us to the beach rescue,” he said.
The rescue team washed the wound with vinegar before taking Lakubanets to a clinic and then to the hospital.
Despite the efforts of medical teams in Langkawi and Alor Setar, Lakubanets was pronounced dead at 9.46am.
Nikita said the family appreciated all the help provided and had no intention of taking legal action, hoping instead that the tragedy serves as a warning to beachgoers about box jellyfish dangers.
His wife, Olga, 32, described Lakubanets as a loving and helpful child, saying, “He is our hero … our little angel. Every day we tell him we love him and he always said he loves us back.”
The couple plan to bring Lakubanets’ ashes back to Russia after cremation.
It was earlier reported that Lakubanets received initial treatment at Sultanah Maliha Hospital in Langkawi before being transferred to the HSB intensive care unit.
Box jellyfish are among the most poisonous marine life, with venom delivered by contact with their tentacles. Their sting can be fatal if not treated immediately.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Hong Kong construction, catering sectors pessimistic despite dip in unemployment
data.description:
Hong Kong’s construction and catering sectors remain pessimistic about their recovery, despite a decline in unemployment rates, citing a lack of new private development projects and unattractive wages for junior positions in the food and beverage industry.
Two industry representatives made the remarks on Thursday, after the government announced the latest unemployment figure of 3.8 per cent over the past three months, marking the first drop since mid-2023. This is 0.1 per cent lower than the July to September 3.9 per cent figure, which was a three-year high.
Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, said that the unemployment rate for the construction sector decreased by 0.2 per cent from 7.2 per cent in the previous three-month period.
“However, compared with the previous few rounds of unemployment figures, there are still fluctuations and they remain at a generally high level, so we cannot confidently say that the industry has recovered,” he told a radio show.
“If we compare the figures year on year, based on the number of construction permits issued, the number of projects this year has dropped significantly.”
He noted that the industry was facing a gap in construction projects and was worried that if the lack of private developments persisted, the unemployment rate would rise again.
He said the construction process was divided into three stages, with the pre-construction workers hit hardest by unemployment.
“Some of the sites are in their final stages and working on mechanical and electrical engineering, but without new projects, after these are completed, there would be no jobs in the entire construction line,” he said.
Chau added that a hiatus has already occurred, making it difficult to assess the future trend of unemployment in the construction industry.

He warned of an exodus of unemployed workers who would switch to other industries during the downtime, leading to a talent drain.
But Chau said the Northern Metropolis, the government’s megaproject in the New Territories, could possibly be the “light at the end of the tunnel”, for the industry.
He also said that as the developments started and entered the preliminary engineering stage, they would bring optimism and much-needed private investment to the industry.
“But distant water cannot extinguish the fire at hand,” Chau lamented, as time was still needed for building basic infrastructure such as roads before construction could begin.
For the catering industry, the latest data show that the unemployment rate in the food and beverage service industry has fallen by 0.4 per cent to 6.4 per cent compared with the previous three-month period.
Chiu Kwun-chung, head of the Eating Establishment Employees General Union’s labour rights committee, said that despite the dip, unemployment rates remained relatively high.
“The slight drop may be related to the easing of shutdowns of major chains and restaurants compared with earlier this year, and an improvement in the economy,” he said.
“But another problem we are facing is workers being owed wages, raising concerns that if the economy deteriorates again, some restaurants might once again face closure, leading to unemployment.”
He noted that authorities had cracked down on employers abusing the foreign labour scheme, which unions said led to local workers being fired and replaced.
But Chiu said Chinese food establishments were hit hard and also saw a decline in the number of banquet bookings.
Some employers would also hire foreign workers to serve at banquets, pushing out local part-time workers, he said, while junior positions, which pay less and are more demanding, were difficult to fill.
“For these junior roles, they may need to take on more than what the job description says, and do a little bit of everything, from preparing ingredients to stir-frying,” he said.
“The positions are offering wages between HK$14,000 [US$1,800] and HK$16,000, which is unattractive compared with the expected salary of around HK$20,000, making it difficult to attract people to join the industry.”
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po has earlier said that he expected the city’s unemployment rate to stabilise or even decline as the economy gains positive momentum, pointing to the surge in visitor figures and exports, among other things.

data.title: Evergrande’s iconic Canadian hotel to be sold after default
data.description:
China Evergrande Group’s famous log-cabin hotel in Quebec, Canada, has been placed in receivership after the defaulted real estate developer failed to make payments on its debt.
Fairmont Le Chateau Montebello, which has hosted luminaries including Grace Kelly and Margaret Thatcher, would be put up for sale by PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, the court-appointed receiver, according to documents filed this week on the accounting firm’s website.
Evergrande’s collapse was by far the biggest in a property crisis that dragged down China’s economic growth for years and led to a record spate of distress among developers. The company, which first defaulted in 2021, was once the country’s largest developer by sales.
The firm’s insolvent subsidiary, Millennium Golden Jiachen Hotel Holdings, owes C$58 million (US$41 million) to creditors, including C$11 million to an affiliate of Desjardins Group. The Canadian lender filed a motion to put the hotel up for sale, according to court documents.
The 95-year-old hotel will remain open during the sale process. Billing itself as the world’s largest log cabin, the resort opened as a private club in the depths of the Great Depression and is now managed by Accor’s Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.
The hotel, with more than 211 rooms and suites, is located 134km west of Montreal and includes an 18-hole golf course, a spa and a conference centre. Evergrande bought it in 2014 from Oxford Properties Group, a unit of the Omers pension plan.
Quebec’s Superior Court appointed Philippe Jordan, a partner with PwC, to act as receiver earlier this month. The insolvency process was first reported by the Journal de Montreal.
“We are currently taking the necessary steps to contact stakeholders, including Millennium and Accor,” Jordan said in a phone interview. “Ultimately, the action plan will include putting the hotel up for sale.”
Last week, liquidators for the delisted China Evergrande Group asked “selected bidders” to submit offers by the end of the month for a property-management unit they have been seeking to sell, according to a filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
The deadline for the offer, as well as identifying bidders for China Evergrande’s stake in Evergrande Property Services (EPS), came as creditors sought to recover assets from the Guangzhou-based developer, which collapsed under the weight of more than US$300 billion in liabilities.

data.title: Beijing vows ‘zero tolerance’ ahead of Legco poll as Hong Kong’s ICAC charges 3
data.description:
Beijing’s national security arm in Hong Kong has pledged to adopt a “zero tolerance” approach in safeguarding next month’s Legislative Council election, as the city’s anti-corruption agency charged three people for allegedly inciting others to boycott the poll.
The Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) charged three individuals for allegedly reposting online messages that urged others not to vote and, on Thursday, issued arrest warrants for two men suspected of making such posts.
The law enforcement action coincided with a renewed warning from the Office for Safeguarding National Security against any attempts to disrupt the December 7 election, issued on the same day.
“Some anti-China forces and external forces have not given up their evil intentions and continued to incite others – openly and covertly – to boycott the election,” a spokesman of the Office for Safeguarding National Security said.
He accused these forces of spreading rumours and disrupting electoral order under the guise of “democracy and freedom”, with a “true aim” of dragging Hong Kong back into political chaos.
The Beijing office noted that the city’s law enforcement and judicial authorities had taken firm action against any attempts to interfere with the election.
“The Office for Safeguarding National Security will adopt a ‘zero tolerance’ approach in safeguarding election security and maintaining election order,” the spokesman said, adding that it would also resolutely support local authorities in enforcing the national security law and other legislation to ensure the smooth running of the coming poll.

The three people charged by the ICAC – two men and a woman, aged 55 to 61 – each face one count of engaging in illegal conduct to incite another person not to vote by activity in public during an election period, under the Elections (Corrupt and Illegal Conduct) Ordinance. They were due to appear at West Kowloon Court on Thursday afternoon.
Separately, the ICAC has issued arrest warrants for self-exiled activist Alan Keung Ka-wai and Tong Wai-hung, accusing them of publishing social media posts urging others not to vote.
Keung is also separately wanted for suspected subversion under the national security law since July, with a HK$200,000 (US$25,676) bounty on his head.
Both central and local authorities have repeatedly warned against any attempts to disrupt the Legco election, the second to be held under Beijing’s “patriots-only” electoral overhaul.
On Tuesday, national security police arrested a 68-year-old man on suspicion of posting social media messages encouraging others not to vote or to cast invalid ballots in next month’s election.

data.title: Huge security operation in place for Africa’s first G20 summit
data.description:
South African police and army units held a parade on Wednesday involving helicopters, K-9 dog units and officers on motorcycles in a show of force ahead of expected protests around this weekend’s Group of 20 world leaders summit in Johannesburg.
South Africa has deployed 3,500 extra police officers and put the army on standby under its National Joint Operational and Intelligence Structure - a body that brings police, army and intelligence services together under one command to provide security for major events.
Deputy national commissioner for policing Lieutenant General Tebello Mosikili told reporters this week that authorities were expecting protests in Johannesburg and other major South African cities.
“We will allow that right (to protest) to be exercised,” she said. “But within the proper directives and proper confines of the law.”

South African police said they have designated specific areas for protesters to gather near the summit venue, an exhibition centre next to the country’s biggest soccer stadium. The head of Airports Company South Africa, which runs the main international airports, said it had set up “speakers’ corners” in airports where protesters would be “kindly” taken by security if they stage demonstrations when world leaders arrive.
The two-day summit opens on Saturday and is expected to attract leaders and top diplomats from more than 40 countries as well as global institutions like the United Nations, the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund and the World Trade Organization.
Demonstrations were expected from anti-capitalists, climate activists, women’s rights campaigners, anti-migrant groups and others, some of which are raising South Africa’s own problems with poverty and inequality.
A trade union representing members of South Africa’s Afrikaner white minority has already stoked controversy by putting up billboards around Johannesburg that say: “Welcome to the most RACE-REGULATED country in the world”. One of the boards was taken down by city authorities, prompting the Afrikaner trade union, Solidarity, to threaten legal action.
The billboards were in reference to South Africa’s affirmative action laws that advance opportunities for black people and have become part of a diplomatic fallout between South Africa and the United States.
US President Donald Trump would not attend the G20 summit in a boycott over his claims that South Africa’s black-led government was pursuing racist, anti-white policies and violently persecuting its Afrikaner minority. Trump’s claims have been widely rejected as baseless, but the US government boycott threatened to undermine the first G20 summit in Africa.

Other groups hoped to use the opportunity to draw attention to a myriad of issues.
The Women for Change advocacy group was calling for a national shutdown on Friday, the eve of the summit. It was asking women to boycott work on the day in protest at South Africa’s extremely high rates of violence against women and femicide.
“Because until South Africa stops burying a woman every 2.5 hours, the G20 cannot speak of growth and progress,” Women for Change said.
A South African anti-immigration group will protest against the joblessness and poverty in the country, its leader said, with South Africa’s 31 per cent unemployment rate one of the highest in the world.
A coalition of groups protesting against climate change and wealth inequality have organised an alternative summit in another part of Johannesburg starting on Thursday, saying the G20 gathering is “for the rich”.
Authorities in Johannesburg have also embarked on a major clear-up and repair operation ahead of the summit to tackle some of the broken-down infrastructure that plagues South Africa’s biggest city.

South African President Cyril Ramaphosa joined the effort last week, when he threw on a pair of green overalls and helped with some of the work in the township of Soweto just a few miles from where the summit will be held.
The multimillion-dollar operation for a two-day meeting of politicians has been viewed with cynicism by many in Johannesburg who have become accustomed to broken street lights, cracked and potholed roads and deteriorating services that lead to water and electricity blackouts.
“Us hosting this G20, I don’t really think it’s going to be beneficial for South Africans, for the ordinary South Africans. It’s just a way to waste money, if I can say,” Johannesburg resident Lerato Lelusa said.

data.title: Sandy Lam, Placido Domingo, Zhou Shen: 5 China Greater Bay Area events in December 2025
data.description:
From major pop stars to classical music icons, the Greater Bay Area (GBA) will play host to some exciting musical acts in December, including a beloved Hong Kong singer, who will perform a New Year’s Eve show.
Read on to see what is coming up in Shenzhen and Guangzhou.
1. Concerto di Placido Domingo: Guangzhou

Legendary opera singer Placido Domingo is returning to the GBA after his October 2024 Macau concert.
This time, he will perform at the Zaha Hadid-designed Guangzhou Opera House, supported by the Cuban-American soprano Monica Conesa and accompanied by the Guangzhou Symphony Orchestra.
The programme is divided into two halves: the first will feature arias from Italian classics such as Tosca and Il trovatore, while the second will be made up of more modern pieces.
The Spaniard, who has won various Grammy and Latin Grammy Awards, has sung in over 4,000 performances throughout his six-decade-long career.
Date: December 2
Venue: Guangzhou Opera House
How to get there: Guangzhou Metro Line 3 to Canton Tower Station, or Line 3 or 5 to Zhujiang New Town Station
Tickets: 380 to 1380 yuan via the venue’s website or Damai
2. Zhou Shen’s Concert Tour: Shenzhen

From China’s Hunan province, Zhou will perform in Shenzhen as part of his tour called “ShenShen”, which sounds like the word for “deeply” in Mandarin.
The tour, which began in Shanghai in May, coincides with the 10th anniversary of his first studio single, “The Rose and the Deer” (2015).
The concerts feature 3D visual effects, creating an “emotional universe” to accompany his four-octave range.
After growing up in central and southwestern China, Zhou studied Bel canto at the Lviv National Music Academy in Ukraine. In 2014, at the age of 21, he took part in the third season of The Voice of China, a singing competition show, where he garnered national acclaim.
Dates: December 6 and 7
Venue: Shenzhen Universiade Sports Centre
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 3 to Dayun Centre
Tickets: 399 to 1,699 yuan via Damai, Piaoxingqiu or Ctrip
3. The Vienna Berlin Music Club – Philharmonix in Concert: Shenzhen

Comprising seven musicians, including three from the Vienna Philharmonic and two from the Berlin Philharmonic orchestras, Philharmonix will play in Shenzhen to kick off the China leg of its East Asia tour.
Calling itself “the Vienna Berlin Music Club”, the group stands out among other classical music repertoires because of its fun-loving approach to performance, as the members creatively mix in sounds from other genres such as folk, jazz, Latin and pop.
Date: December 11
Venue: Symphony Hall, Shenzhen Concert Hall
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 4 to Children’s Palace Station
Tickets: 80 to 580 yuan via the venue’s website
4. Borodin Quartet 80th Anniversary Concerts: Shostakovich Complete String Quartets

The Borodin Quartet will be in Guangzhou to perform the complete quartet cycle of its one-time close collaborator, Russian composer Dmitri Shostakovich, 50 years after his death.
Founded in Moscow in 1945, the Borodin Quartet is among the world’s oldest and most celebrated string quartets, one that has a long history of performing in China.
This stop on the quartet’s 80th-anniversary tour marks its fifth appearance at the Xinghai Concert Hall.
Dates: December 20, 21, 23, 24 and 26
Venue: Xinghai Concert Hall
How to get there: Guangzhou Metro Line 6 to Shaheding Station
Tickets: 180 to 380 yuan via the venue’s website
5. Sandy Lam ‘Resonance’ 2025 Concert Tour: Shenzhen

On December 31, Sandy Lam Yik-lin will perform a New Year’s Eve concert in Shenzhen. It is a full-circle moment for her “Resonance” tour, which began in the same stadium in February.
Marking 40 years since the 1985 release of her self-titled debut album, the Hong Kong songstress has resumed touring in the past year after a long hiatus, proving her enduring popularity by filling stadiums in nearly 30 cities across mainland China and Macau.
Date: December 31
Venue: Shenzhen Bay Sports Centre
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 11 to Houhai Station
Tickets: TBA

data.title: Europe looks for common ground with Indo-Pacific nations bruised by US-China rivalry
data.description:
Dozens of senior officials and diplomats from across Asia, Africa and the Pacific will touch down in Brussels on Thursday, as the European Union tries to convince them it is a more reliable partner than the United States and China.
More than 70 delegations will attend the bloc’s fourth Indo-Pacific forum, with more than 50 ministers or vice-ministers expected at talks on Friday, EU officials said. Crucially, neither Beijing or Washington are invited.
“There was no invitation to special guests – this is only for our Indo-Pacific partners. It is an occasion to discuss with them in an open and frank way. With the US, Canada, Norway and others, we already discuss with them the Indo-Pacific,” said one official involved in the planning, referring to countries previously invited to the forum.
A second said that “China has never been invited – it’s not about China”, even if the discussion was likely to turn to Beijing’s stronghold on critical minerals.
“China has not attended in the past but this doesn’t mean the principle of the forum is against anyone. We have a lot of dedicated and high-level channels with China … dialogue with China has its own momentum,” they added.

With many countries involved battered by US President Donald Trump’s trade tariffs and bruised from being caught in the crosshairs of the superpower rivalry, organisers are hopeful that this forum might be more fruitful than previous editions.
In previous years, the EU has struggled even to get its own people to attend. Two years ago in Stockholm, barely half the bloc’s 27 foreign ministers showed up, despite the forum being bolted onto the end of a meeting that almost all of them attended. Organisers declined to say how many would be there this time around.
Last year in Belgium, meanwhile, there was a slanging match over “double standards”, when ministers from countries such as Indonesia and Sri Lanka accused their hosts of failing to apply the same rigorous critique to Israel’s bombardment of Gaza that it does to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.
“I appeal to all of you to listen to your heart and do the right thing, stop the atrocities in Gaza, in Palestine,” then-Indonesian foreign minister Retno Marsudi told the forum, adding that “consistency with values and actions reflect sincerity of moral high ground … it shows who we actually are”.
But this time around, observers and officials sense an opening for Europe: Trump’s tariffs and Beijing’s export controls have set off a global scramble for diversification. This could be advanced by Trump’s decision to boycott the first G20 summit to be held in Africa this weekend.
The US has threatened to punish South Africa if it attempts to arrange a leaders’ statement in its absence and EU officials involved in the planning described a vacuum that was unfilled. Surprisingly, they said, Beijing had not attempted to move into the space.
Accordingly, there are no plans for EU leaders to meet Chinese Premier Li Qiang in South Africa, with officials saying there was no point meeting until some of the concerns raised by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen with Li at the UN General Assembly in September or by European Council chief Antonio Costa at the Asean summit in Kuala Lumpur in October are addressed.
Instead, sources said, the Europeans were rallying around hosts South Africa in a bid to make the summit a success.

On the Indo-Pacific front, the EU hopes to soon conclude free-trade deals with several attendees, including India and Indonesia, while plans are afoot to upgrade its ties with the Asean members to a “comprehensive, strategic partnership” on the 50th anniversary of relations in 2027.
A survey this year by the ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute – a think tank – found that for Asean members, the EU is the preferred hedging partner in the superpower rivalry, taking 36.3 per cent of the vote, even as Europe’s reputation continues to suffer over the Gaza issue.
Under the new Indonesian presidency of Prabowo Subianto, the “tone has changed”, said Dewi Fortuna Anwar, a political scientist and chair of the Jakarta-based Habibie Centre. Before he took office, Prabowo regularly bashed Europe, but Trump had thrust them closer together, she said.
“Throughout his career and throughout his campaign, Prabowo pointed out that the EU has no moral standing on human rights and on Asia due to its colonial past and silence and complicity of Israel’s war in Gaza,” Anwar said.
“During his foreign policy speech before he was elected president, he took a question from an EU person in the audience, and told them Indonesia doesn’t need Europe. But then he switched completely. I think we have to be thankful to Trump … the EU and Indonesia now need each other. The tone has changed completely.”
The summit’s success, however, may depend on whether the hosts can leave some of their core issues at the door, including Ukraine.
Two years ago, some attendees were vexed when Ukraine’s then-foreign minister Dmytro Kuleba showed up unannounced. One visiting official described it at the time to the Post as “an ambush”. Another said they had “not come for a lecture”.
It will also provide a test for Kaja Kallas, the bloc’s top diplomat who will chair Thursday’s dinner and Friday’s talks, and for whom Russia is clearly the No 1 foreign policy issue. To the extent that Kallas has focused on Asia during her one-year tenure, it has largely been through the lens of the war.
At an event in Brussels on Wednesday, Kallas said that without China’s support, Russia would have already lost the war and warned that Europe’s economic entanglement with Beijing was constraining its ability to punish Moscow.
China “might also hurt you, and that’s the problem”, said Kallas, according to Bloomberg. “If you’re not willing to pay the price that they are going to impose on you, then it’s hard to act,” she added.
Moreover, a recent discussion of the EU’s foreign ministers on the Indo-Pacific – chaired by Kallas – lasted just nine minutes.

European experts are sceptical as to whether the event in Brussels will deliver much. Stefania Benaglia, an independent EU foreign policy adviser, said the bloc was on a steep learning curve.
“We’re learning how to do foreign policy – but not strategic foreign policy. It mostly happens as a reaction to events,” Benaglia said.
“I have low expectations – this is the first summit with a new [high representative] who doesn’t have Indo-Pacific high up in her priorities. So it will be a little bit of getting to know each other,” she said.
“I would not expect ground-shaking outcomes – there is not the time or this is not the format for that. Instead the fact that the meeting is taking place, the discussions have not been discontinued – that is the outcome.”
